purpose: Reference playbook for the Insurer Defense agent and Patient-side agents in the Denial Defense multi-agent system. Maps the most-appealable CARC denial codes to the insurer reasoning patterns, patient counter-arguments, and weak spots that drive successful appeals.

usage.insurer_defense_agent: On round 1, identify the CARC code(s) in the user's denial letter. Load the matching entries. Use 'typical_insurer_arguments' and the 'category' to construct the strongest adversarial critique of the patient's draft appeal. On round 2, attack the weakest counter the patient deployed.
usage.patient_agents: Use 'common_patient_counters', 'weak_spots_for_insurer', and 'typical_evidence_needed' to construct rebuttal arguments. Cite specific policy language wherever possible — generic appeals fail.
usage.supervisor: Use 'appealability' to set expectations on the synthesized verdict — a CARC 50 medical necessity denial with strong evidence has very different odds than a CARC 119 benefit max.

data_sources: CMS Medicare Claims Processing Manual chapters on appeals
CA DMHC Independent Medical Review dataset 2001-2026 (analyzed Findings field across 42,750 cases)
Domain expertise from EDI 837 claims adjudication

appealability_scale.high: Successful appeals are common (>40%) when evidence is well-prepared. Most worth the patient's effort.
appealability_scale.medium: Appeals succeed (15-40%) but require specific evidence. Patient must be diligent.
appealability_scale.low: Appeals rarely succeed (<15%) without unusual circumstances. Set expectations honestly.

CARC_50.plain_english: These are non-covered services because this is not deemed a 'medical necessity' by the payer.
CARC_50.frequency_in_imr_data: very_common — this is the single most-appealed code in the CA DMHC dataset, especially the Medical Necessity IMR Type
common_paired_rarc: N115 (decision based on local coverage determination)
typical_insurer_arguments: Patient does not meet [specific criteria] in medical policy [policy number]
Conservative or first-line treatment has not been tried and failed (step therapy)
Clinical evidence does not support the requested intervention for this patient's specific presentation
Documented diagnosis does not match the indications listed in the coverage policy
Requested service can be provided at a lower level of care
Patient's clinical picture is stable; intervention is not urgent
common_patient_counters: Cite the exact policy criteria and demonstrate item-by-item how the patient meets each one
Document conservative treatment failure with dates, dosages, and clinical outcomes
Submit peer-reviewed evidence (PubMed) supporting the intervention for the specific indication
Include a Letter of Medical Necessity from the treating physician with clinical reasoning
Reference comorbidity or exception clauses that apply to this patient
If denied at a higher level of care, document why lower levels are inappropriate (failed prior attempts, clinical risk)
weak_spots_for_insurer: Policy language is often ambiguous; argue plain-meaning interpretation favorable to the patient
When multiple criteria are listed with 'any of the following,' meeting only ONE is sufficient
Comorbidity exception clauses are commonly buried in section subheadings and ignored by reviewers
Policy citations must match the policy version in effect on the date of service — insurers sometimes apply newer/older versions incorrectly
If the denial cites a Medicare LCD/NCD, check whether the patient's plan is actually subject to it (commercial plans often have separate criteria)
typical_evidence_needed: Treating physician's Letter of Medical Necessity (signed, on letterhead)
Clinical chart notes covering the relevant treatment history
1-3 peer-reviewed citations supporting the indication
The exact medical policy PDF in effect at the date of service
Documentation of failed alternatives if step therapy is at issue
CARC_50.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Approximately 50-60% of Medical Necessity IMR cases in CA DMHC data are overturned when well-documented

CARC_197.plain_english: Precertification, authorization, notification, or pre-treatment requirement absent.
CARC_197.frequency_in_imr_data: common — often paired with retroactive authorization disputes
common_paired_rarc: N210 (alert: you may appeal this decision)
N640 (alert: prior auth on file does not match service rendered)
typical_insurer_arguments: Patient or provider failed to obtain prior authorization before the service was rendered
Authorization was obtained but for a different CPT/HCPCS code than the one billed
Authorization expired before the date of service
Service was provided outside the scope of what was authorized (e.g., different facility, different provider)
Notification timeline was missed (inpatient admissions typically have 24-48 hour notification rules)
common_patient_counters: If the service was emergent or urgent, argue that prior auth requirements do not apply (cite the No Surprises Act for emergency services)
Document that prior auth WAS obtained — include authorization number, date, and contact log
If auth was for a different code, argue medical equivalence and request retroactive auth
Argue that the provider's office submitted the request and any failure is administrative, not the patient's fault
Cite state-specific retroactive authorization rules (many states require insurers to grant retro auth in certain circumstances)
weak_spots_for_insurer: Many states have retroactive authorization requirements that insurers routinely overlook
Emergency and urgent care services are exempt from prior auth in most plans — check the plan document
If the patient was not informed of the auth requirement, ERISA notice requirements may apply
If the auth request was submitted but the insurer failed to respond within their stated timeline, the request is often deemed approved
typical_evidence_needed: Phone log or fax confirmation showing auth was requested
Auth number if obtained (even for a different code)
Provider's office documentation of the auth submission
Plan document language on emergency/urgent exemptions
State insurance regulations on retroactive authorization
CARC_197.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Variable — depends heavily on whether the patient can show good-faith effort to obtain auth

CARC_96_N130_experimental.rarc_specifier: N130 (consult plan benefit documents/guidelines for information about restrictions for this service)
CARC_96_N130_experimental.plain_english: Non-covered charge(s) — specifically when the insurer classifies the treatment as experimental or investigational.
CARC_96_N130_experimental.frequency_in_imr_data: common — this is the Experimental/Investigational IMR Type in the CA DMHC data
typical_insurer_arguments: The treatment is considered experimental or investigational for this patient's condition
FDA approval does not exist for this specific indication (off-label use)
Insufficient peer-reviewed evidence of safety and efficacy for the patient population
Treatment is not the standard of care per accepted clinical guidelines
Clinical trial data is preliminary or limited to small studies
common_patient_counters: Cite recent peer-reviewed studies (within 3-5 years) supporting safety and efficacy
Reference specialty society guidelines that endorse the treatment (ASMBS, WPATH, ASAM, NCCN, etc.)
If FDA-approved for a related indication, argue therapeutic equivalence
Document that no covered standard treatment exists or that standards have failed for this patient
Cite other insurers that cover the treatment (sets precedent the policy is out of step)
Reference the specific patient's clinical factors that make this treatment indicated
weak_spots_for_insurer: 'Experimental' classifications often lag clinical practice by 5-10 years
If specialty societies endorse the treatment as standard, the experimental label is rebuttable
Off-label use is legal and frequently medically indicated — insurers cannot deny purely on FDA labeling for many drug classes
External review reviewers (IMR) overturn at meaningful rates when treatment has emerging evidence base
typical_evidence_needed: 3-5 peer-reviewed studies, ideally including at least one RCT or systematic review
Specialty society guideline or position statement
Documentation of failed standard treatments
Letter from a specialist who has experience with the treatment
CARC_96_N130_experimental.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Approximately 30-45% in CA DMHC Experimental/Investigational IMR cases — lower than Medical Necessity but still substantial

CARC_167.frequency_in_imr_data: moderate — often a coding/documentation issue rather than a true coverage dispute
typical_insurer_arguments: The submitted diagnosis code does not match a covered indication for this procedure
ICD-10 code is too non-specific to establish medical necessity
Diagnosis is excluded from coverage per the patient's specific plan (e.g., cosmetic exclusions, infertility exclusions in some plans)
The diagnosis-to-procedure linkage required by the policy is not documented
common_patient_counters: Provide additional clinical documentation supporting a more specific or appropriate ICD-10 code
Ask the provider to resubmit with a corrected or additional diagnosis code
Cite the policy language showing the diagnosis IS covered for the procedure
If the exclusion is plan-specific, verify the patient's plan version and effective date
Argue that the diagnosis falls within the covered category (e.g., medically necessary vs cosmetic)
weak_spots_for_insurer: Coding errors are common and often resolved by the provider resubmitting with a more specific code
ICD-10 hierarchy means parent codes often imply child codes — the policy may cover the parent
Insurer's diagnosis-procedure crosswalks are sometimes outdated relative to current coding guidelines
Cosmetic/medical distinction is contested for many procedures (e.g., gender-affirming care, reconstructive surgery)
typical_evidence_needed: Updated provider documentation with specific diagnosis
ICD-10 code reference showing appropriate linkage
Plan document showing the diagnosis category is covered
Specialty society statement on the medical (not cosmetic) nature of the indication
CARC_167.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Often resolved at internal appeal before reaching IMR; high reversal when coding correction is possible

CARC_252.plain_english: An attachment/other documentation is required to adjudicate this claim/service.
CARC_252.frequency_in_imr_data: moderate — most resolved before external review
common_paired_rarc: MA130 (claim contains incomplete and/or invalid information)
typical_insurer_arguments: Required clinical documentation was not submitted with the claim
Operative report, pathology report, or treatment plan is missing
Documentation does not support the level of service billed
Required attachments (e.g., medical necessity letter, imaging reports) are absent
common_patient_counters: Simply submit the missing documentation — this is the most easily resolved category
Request itemized list of what's missing from the insurer
Have the provider submit a corrected claim with all required attachments
Document the timeline to ensure submission within the appeal window
weak_spots_for_insurer: Insurers must specify what's missing — vague 'needs more info' denials are appealable on procedural grounds
Once requested documentation is submitted, the clock restarts and the denial reason effectively disappears
If documentation was previously submitted and the insurer lost it, that's the insurer's error
typical_evidence_needed: Whatever specific document the insurer requested
Cover letter listing all documents being resubmitted
Proof of timely original submission if applicable
CARC_252.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Very high once documentation is provided; rarely reaches external review

CARC_16.plain_english: Claim/service lacks information or has submission/billing error(s).
CARC_16.frequency_in_imr_data: low — typically administrative, not appealed externally
typical_insurer_arguments: Claim submission is missing required fields (NPI, modifier, place of service)
Patient identifier does not match the enrollment record
Coding is internally inconsistent (e.g., procedure code does not match place of service)
Date of service is missing or invalid
common_patient_counters: Coordinate with the provider's billing office to resubmit the corrected claim
Request the specific field that's deficient
Verify the patient's enrollment status was active on the date of service
Almost always resolved without a clinical appeal — this is an administrative fix
weak_spots_for_insurer: The insurer must specify which field is deficient — generic CARC 16 denials are procedurally weak
If the missing information is something the insurer already has, the denial is improper
Resubmission with the correction usually voids the original denial
typical_evidence_needed: Corrected claim from the provider
Documentation of patient enrollment on date of service
CARC_16.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Almost always resolved at the claim resubmission level

CARC_204.plain_english: This service/equipment/drug is not covered under the patient's current benefit plan.
CARC_204.frequency_in_imr_data: moderate — appealability depends entirely on whether the exclusion is firm or arguable
typical_insurer_arguments: The specific service is excluded from the patient's plan (e.g., 'cosmetic procedures,' 'infertility services,' 'adult dental,' 'experimental treatments')
Plan does not include the relevant benefit category at all
Service falls outside the geographic scope or network of the plan
Patient has not met preconditions for the benefit (e.g., waiting period)
common_patient_counters: Argue the service falls into a covered category rather than an excluded one (e.g., reconstructive vs cosmetic)
Cite federal mandates (ACA Essential Health Benefits, mental health parity, No Surprises Act) that override plan exclusions
Reference state-mandated benefits that apply to fully-insured plans
If the exclusion language is ambiguous, argue plain-meaning interpretation
For self-insured ERISA plans, check whether the Summary Plan Description actually contains the cited exclusion
weak_spots_for_insurer: Plan exclusions are not unlimited — they must comply with federal law (ACA, MHPAEA, NSA, ADA)
Mental health parity violations are common — services restricted only in MH/SUD are often illegal
State-mandated benefits override plan exclusions for fully-insured plans (not self-insured ERISA plans)
Exclusion language is often vague enough to permit category arguments (cosmetic vs reconstructive is a frequent battleground)
typical_evidence_needed: Patient's actual plan document or Summary Plan Description
Federal law citations (ACA EHB, MHPAEA, NSA, Section 1557)
State insurance code citations if applicable
Medical documentation establishing the service is medically necessary (not the excluded category)
CARC_204.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Lower than medical necessity overall; higher when federal mandate or category dispute is at issue

CARC_119.plain_english: Benefit maximum for this time period or occurrence has been reached.
CARC_119.frequency_in_imr_data: low — hard limits are rarely overturned at IMR
common_paired_rarc: N362 (number of days or units of service exceeds acceptable maximum)
typical_insurer_arguments: Patient has reached the annual/lifetime visit limit for this benefit category
Allowed units (PT visits, mental health visits, days of inpatient care) have been exhausted
Dollar maximum on a specific benefit category has been reached
common_patient_counters: Argue the cap violates mental health parity (MHPAEA) if it applies only to behavioral health
Cite the No Surprises Act if the limit is being applied to emergency services
Request a benefit extension on medical necessity grounds (some plans allow this)
If the cap is being applied incorrectly (counting visits that weren't actually used), document the actual utilization
ACA prohibits lifetime and annual dollar limits on essential health benefits — verify this isn't being violated
weak_spots_for_insurer: Mental health visit caps are illegal if no equivalent medical/surgical cap exists (MHPAEA)
ACA prohibits annual and lifetime dollar limits on EHBs
If the cap counts denied/disputed visits, those should be excluded
Some plans allow medical necessity extensions but don't inform patients
typical_evidence_needed: Plan document showing the actual benefit limit language
Utilization record showing what was actually used vs counted
Federal law citation if parity or ACA limits are at issue
CARC_119.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Low unless a federal parity or ACA violation is involved

CARC_242.plain_english: Services not provided by network/primary care providers.
CARC_242.frequency_in_imr_data: moderate — often resolved via No Surprises Act protections
common_paired_rarc: N448 (this drug/service/supply is not included in the fee schedule)
typical_insurer_arguments: Provider is out-of-network and patient did not have an in-network referral
Service should have been provided by an in-network alternative
Patient chose to use an out-of-network provider despite available in-network options
Reimbursement is at the OON benefit level, not in-network
common_patient_counters: Cite the No Surprises Act (NSA) — emergency services, OON facility-based providers at in-network facilities, and air ambulance are protected
Argue network inadequacy — if no in-network provider was available within the required time/distance, OON services must be covered at in-network rates
Document that the patient was not given notice the provider was OON (NSA requires advance notice and consent)
Cite continuity of care provisions if the patient was already in active treatment when the provider went OON
Reference state-specific network adequacy rules
weak_spots_for_insurer: No Surprises Act (effective 2022) substantially limits OON balance billing — many denials predate or ignore NSA protections
Network adequacy violations are increasingly enforced by state regulators
If the patient received care at an in-network facility, ALL providers there are protected under NSA regardless of their individual network status
Insurers must provide accurate provider directories — directory errors that led patients to OON providers shift liability
typical_evidence_needed: Documentation that the service was emergent, at an in-network facility, or otherwise NSA-protected
Provider directory printout showing the provider was listed as in-network (if applicable)
Timeline showing in-network alternatives were not available or appropriate
Plan document on network adequacy and continuity of care provisions
CARC_242.imr_overturn_rate_observed: High when NSA protections apply; moderate otherwise

CARC_B7.plain_english: This provider was not certified/eligible to be paid for this procedure/service on this date of service.
CARC_B7.frequency_in_imr_data: low — often resolved via credentialing fix rather than IMR
typical_insurer_arguments: Provider was not credentialed with the plan on the date of service
Provider's specialty does not match the service rendered
Provider's license was inactive, suspended, or restricted on the date of service
Provider has not enrolled in the network for this benefit category
common_patient_counters: Document that the patient reasonably believed the provider was in-network (provider directory, prior visits, etc.)
If provider credentialing was in process, argue the service should be covered retroactively upon completion
Cite continuity of care if the patient was already in treatment
Argue the patient cannot be held responsible for the provider's credentialing status
Request the provider be credentialed retroactively if eligible
weak_spots_for_insurer: Patients cannot verify credentialing status in real-time and should not bear the cost of insurer-side credentialing gaps
Provider directory accuracy laws place the burden on the insurer to keep listings current
If the provider has been credentialed before and after the date of service, gaps are often administrative
Continuity of care protections apply when patients are actively in treatment
typical_evidence_needed: Provider directory screenshot showing the provider was listed
Prior EOBs showing the provider was paid by the same insurer for similar services
Documentation of the patient's good-faith belief in network status
Continuity of care documentation if applicable
CARC_B7.imr_overturn_rate_observed: Variable — depends on whether the credentialing issue is a real gap or an administrative artifact

no_surprises_act.key_argument: Patients cannot be balance-billed for protected services. Insurer must pay at in-network rates.

applies_to: any restriction on MH/SUD benefits that is more restrictive than medical/surgical benefits
mental_health_parity_addiction_equity_ac.key_argument: Parity violations include quantitative limits (visit caps), prior auth burdens, and network adequacy disparities. Insurers must justify any differences.

applies_to: 10 EHB categories including ambulatory, emergency, hospitalization, MH/SUD, prescription drugs, rehabilitative, lab, preventive, pediatric
aca_essential_health_benefits.key_argument: Annual and lifetime dollar limits on EHBs are prohibited. Plans must cover EHBs in non-grandfathered individual and small group markets.

applies_to: any plan receiving federal financial assistance
section_1557_nondiscrimination.key_argument: Prohibits discrimination on the basis of race, color, national origin, sex (including gender identity and pregnancy), age, or disability. Frequently cited in gender-affirming care appeals.

erisa_full_and_fair_review.key_argument: Denial notice must include the specific reasons, plan provisions cited, and a description of any additional material needed. Vague denials are appealable on procedural grounds alone.

synthesis_notes_for_agents.ranking_arguments: When multiple counter-arguments are available, prioritize: (1) federal law citations, (2) policy-specific exception clauses, (3) medical evidence, (4) procedural deficiencies in the denial itself, (5) general medical necessity arguments.
synthesis_notes_for_agents.tone_guidance: Appeal letters should be firm, specific, and clinical. Avoid emotional language. Cite exact policy language. Reference dates, codes, and specific clinical findings. Avoid making legal threats; instead reference the law dispassionately.
synthesis_notes_for_agents.structural_template: 1. Identify the patient, plan, claim, and date of service. 2. Acknowledge the denial reason cited. 3. State the basis for appeal in one sentence. 4. Present evidence point-by-point matching the cited policy. 5. Request specific action (overturn, retro auth, payment at specific rate). 6. Note appeal rights including external review.
